takeaway: A brand is more than a logo: "A brand is a name, term, design, symbol or any other feature that distinguishes one seller's goods or service from those of other sellers," and beyond those marks it exists as a psychological construct — perceptions and expectations — in the customer's mind.

Brand marketing builds long-term demand while performance marketing harvests it; the memorable framing is "Performance marketing without brand building is like fishing in a lake that nobody restocks. Sooner or later, the fish run out" — based on Binet & Field's 996-campaign IPA analysis.

Brand positioning, popularized by Ries and Trout, creates a distinct mental place for a brand relative to competitors — "an organized system for finding a window in the mind" — and is the strategic decision every other brand expression serves.

Brand management is "the process of controlling how a brand is perceived in the market"; the key distinction is that brand identity is what the organization wants to project, while brand image is the meaning customers actually hold — the two can drift apart.

Marty Neumeier's The Brand Gap defines the gap as "the gap between how a company sees itself and how the public perceives the company," and frames brand identity (the visual and verbal expression) as the bridge, built through differentiation, positioning, personality, and voice.

Brand awareness has two forms — recall (unaided) and recognition (aided) — and matters because "buying decisions cannot begin unless a consumer is first aware of a product category and a brand within that category"; top-of-mind brands win the most consideration.

Byron Sharp's How Brands Grow argues brands grow by mental availability (easy recall when buying) and physical availability (easy to find and buy), not loyalty programs — "successful brands tend to grow by expanding their customer base" rather than maximizing retention.

Distinctive brand assets — logos, colors, characters, jingles — are the recognizable cues that trigger recall; per Sharp's work these "contribute to building mental availability and strengthening a brand's position in the market by building on their salience or prominence in consumers' minds."

Brand equity is the value customers assign a brand from their perceptions; Keller's customer-based model centers on feeling — "CBBE is the value of a brand, based on what customers feel about and think of it" — while Aaker measures awareness, loyalty, perceived quality, associations, and assets.

Brand equity is the value premium a known brand commands: "The owner of a well-known brand name can generate more revenue simply from brand recognition, as consumers perceive the products of well-known brands as better than those of lesser-known brands" — David Aaker's framework lists ten strength attributes.

Keller's CBBE pyramid builds brand equity through four cumulative tiers — Identity (salience), Meaning (performance + imagery), Response (judgments + feelings), Resonance (loyalty); "a brand first becomes salient, then builds meaning through performance and imagery; that meaning shapes customer responses."

Neumeier's The Brand Gap holds that a brand ultimately lives in customer experience — "the ultimate moment of truth for all brands is the customer's experience" — so storytelling and a clear purpose exist to forge an emotional connection, not just to decorate.

Brand storytelling works because narrative outperforms facts for memory and emotion: "Stories are illustrative, easily memorable and allow companies to create stronger emotional bonds with customers," which is why purpose-driven brand narratives build loyalty.

Binet & Field's The Long and the Short of It found the most effective campaigns split budget ~60% brand-building / 40% sales-activation, and that brand-building works best through "pure emotion in most ads for maximum effectiveness" rather than mixing rational and emotional in one ad.

Share of voice (SOV) measures a brand's media spend as a percentage of its category's total; it is a core brand metric because "having a high share of voice can lead to increased awareness, and ultimately, increased sales and market share."

The field's liveliest brand debate: Byron Sharp dismisses Binet & Field's 60:40 rule as resting on "questionable award submission data rather than rigorous scientific methodology," arguing instead for always-on advertising that builds mental availability year-round — a genuine practitioner split.
